candidates.direction_name: Restore The Best Measured PTX Grouping Window On The Accepted Surface
ranking_notes: The last two aggressive probes were informative but materially regressed the live surface.
This restore is ranked first because the loop should not compound two negative structural branches on top of a regressed implementation base.
A return to 24.16-24.18 ms is still only recovery, not evidence that the global search can stop.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 29 and round 30 spent two consecutive rounds on the occupancy-first 128x128 branch and extracted the information we needed. The launch-bounds-only probe proved that the hot-band PTX kernel can be pushed down to 168 registers and up to 24.77% active warps, but it paid for that with a barrier wall. The two-K-stage follow-on then tried to amortize those barriers, but its 43,008 B shared-memory footprint collapsed active warps back to 16.58% and pushed runtime out to 28.20 ms. At this point the branch has consumed its immediate budget. The correct next move is to restore the exact 489574e PTX winner so the search returns to the best correctness-proven surface before it tries the next aggressive family.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Known register-limited plateau on the accepted 128x128 PTX surface, used here as a recovery anchor rather than a discovery move.
candidates.risk: Low. This is the safe recovery path after two measured regressions.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the current 28.200448 ms run
candidates.stop_condition: Treat a restored 24.16-24.18 ms result as recovery only; the next round should attach a fresh structural change rather than replay the same surface again.

candidates.direction_name: Transplant The Half-Panel Register Budget Into The Correct 256x128 Pivot
ranking_notes: Keep this family live because it still represents the largest visible occupancy headroom in repo history.
Rank it below the restore because round 31 should recover from the compounded regressions before reopening another risky structural branch.
candidates.hypothesis: The occupancy-first 128x128 probes were useful precisely because they confirmed the central thesis behind the more aggressive families: escaping the 16.6%-warps plateau changes the machine state materially. The 256x128 half-panel repairs remain the only branch in the repo that ever pushed warps into the low-30% range, even though the historical snapshots were wrong or too synchronization-heavy. After recovery, the next strongest aggressive family is still to start from the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot branch and transplant the low-register staging behavior without reintroducing the broken writer split.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Register footprint and synchronization strategy in the 256x128 hot-band path, plus correctness-sensitive output ownership.
candidates.risk: High. This family still carries correctness history and broader code motion than a 128x128 restore.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop immediately on correctness failure or on another 28-30 ms class regression with no sign of recovered low-register behavior.

candidates.direction_name: Trim Microkernel Barriers Without Reintroducing Shared-Memory Blowup
ranking_notes: This is the right 128x128 follow-on family after recovery, but not the right thing to auto-select while the live surface is still sitting at 28.20 ms.
Its value is that it respects the lesson from round 30: barrier amortization must not come from doubling the hot-band shared-memory footprint.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 29 and round 30 together now fence off both obvious extremes on the 128x128 occupancy-first path. The launch-bounds probe reduced registers successfully but doubled barrier cost. The two-K-stage kernel reduced per-stage synchronization pressure but doubled shared-memory footprint enough to destroy the occupancy gain. That leaves one bounded but still aggressive follow-up family: keep the smaller 22,016 B shared-memory microkernel footprint from round 29 and attack the barrier cost more surgically through wait/sync cadence or export scheduling rather than by introducing a second full K-tile buffer pair.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Barrier cadence inside the single-K 128x128 PTX microkernel while preserving the lower shared-memory footprint.
candidates.risk: High. This is a more manual PTX-microkernel surgery path than the exact restore.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the edit pushes shared memory above the round-29 footprint again or if it fails to lower barrier stall materially from the round-29 10.97% level.

notes: Projected exactly three node_b directions into normalized search candidates.
